item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image could only be sourced back to Getty Images and other stock image websites; as such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Articles like these are the reason I still visit this site. Amazing premise, very well-written, leaves a bunch to imagination.

item.title: Re: Undeniably Original
item.content:encoded:
It's keter because it:

A: Naturally occurs in the human population (thereby making containment efforts focused only on information-denial rather than putting it in a box)
B: Survivors do not remember the incidents, and deaths caused by it are misinterpreted as suicide. This makes getting solid numbers on who was effected impossible, so the Foundation doesn't know how rampant the phenomenon actually is worldwide.
C: The phenomenon is unpredictable of when it will occur.
D: The phenomenon appears to be guided by an external intelligence.

In other words, we have a SCP that can't be physically contained, that appears essentially at random, whose rate of appearance is unknown, and is apparently guided by an unknown intelligence or intelligences who may or may not be hostile to human life and are performing trials for an unknown reason.

That's why it's Keter.

item.title: Undeniably Original
item.content:encoded: Original idea, and not over the top, I like it. It's interesting enough to deserve an upvote, but I don't see why this is a Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Delightfully unsettling and full of mystery, just the way I like it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might be one of my favorite of Series III; didn't realize this was someone's first until I read the comments here. Really, really impressive.

This is a skip I kinda want to film. Both because it's interesting and effective, and also because it would just be so damn cheap to produce. Hmm…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure how optimistic it is, but certainly humanism is part of what is being contested here.

As to why this is Keter, it is a combination of the inability to contain it, the scale of the phenomenon, and the implications of there being an unknown force that can get to anyone and everyone and which seems to be building to something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is strangely optimistic and humanistic for an SCP about a suicide ritual that affects millions of people and no one knew about it until recently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that it's Keter because there is no definitive way to contain it. (Not saying that Runic's idea is wrong, my idea might even go along with Runic's)

Think about the locked box test. How do you lock a globalized phenomenon up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Keter because of the implications of what exactly is happening. Every juror they get is one step closer to a possible extinction scenario.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a little bit of a necro, but if you're really lost the author has more or less explained it in this comment section.

I think it's generally accepted that most good horror isn't blatantly prescriptive. It's easier to be scared when you have to fill in the blanks. That's why this article has appeal; if it were literally an SCP where people show up and interview you for the Rapture and you either pass or fail, it would be too much "tell" and not enough "show".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally loved the article, and loved what the logs did for it. I do have one problem with it; why is this Keter? Shouldn't it just be Euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry. I have to down for this. I can't stand an SCP that I can't clearly understand.

I think I catch the point, and the idea is good…But this SCP makes me confused. I personally insist that a confusing SCP is never a good SCP. SORRY I HAVE TO DOWN BECAUSE I HAVE OCD.

Implying is one of the best way to make an article amazing, but sometimes it only makes your article confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is utterly fantastic, and the very epitome of fridge horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I'm very glad you enjoyed it. I don't want to ruin the fun for anyone but I would say you're pretty close in terms of the purpose of the interviews, slightly less in terms of the criteria. PM me if you want a fuller explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great article, really love the concept of a "voir dire" for some kind of unfathomable trial, presumably regarding the fitness of humanity for some purpose…but what really made me sit up and get nervous was that _._% figure.

0.1% of humanity is the lowest number that percentage allows. Global population passed 7 billion back in 2011. That means that at least seven million people have exhibited this behavior. That's the kind of number that makes me break out in a cold sweat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AMAZING SCP! I logged in just to post this comment and to upvote you- and that's saying something, as I very rarely log in!

I think I have a theory of what's going on. I'll posit my guess in collapsibles, if you want I'll delete them, but tell me if I am warm.

The purpose of the interviews
– hide block


It's the Rapture, in a sense. Non-denominational, to account for all of the cultures across the world being affected. They interpret this supernatural being on a personal level, that no one else can see. The interview process is basically to select people to join in the supernatural afterlife, perhaps as the jury to help "judge" the rest of the world.

The criteria for which people are affected/selected
– hide block


The supernatural force that is communicating to these people, prefers people who aren't vain about their own existence. These people do not believe themselves to be elevated on a fundamental level above their fellow man, any more than what they themselves have created for themselves. The selected people possess compassion for all living things. The selected people realize their place in the world is small regardless of what ever happens to them. Those who are accepted are accepted because they are the individuals with the greatest capacity for being conscious- conscious of their roles, of their lives, of the world around them, of how much they don't know about things and how willing they are to admit that to people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most authors use █.█% (or whatever) to avoid this confusion, not ███%.

If, on the other hand, you're going for ambiguity… State that a minimum lower bound of a small blackboxed percentage has been determined to be affected, but that there is no upper bound determined. Could be anywhere from 0.1% - 100%. Same effect, less confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Decimal places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great, only criticism is this sentence

SCP-2055 is a behavioural phenomenon that has been observed in ███% of the global population.

That number can only be one thing, I suggest you either uncensor it or make it two digits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. Only quibble:

behavioural phenomenon that has been observed in ███% of the global

There really isn't a point of redacting this, it can only be 100%. I don't think you can really say, for instance, 130% of the population, so I think it should either be unredacted or changed to two digits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor nitpick: I'm not sure I'm entirely behind the amount of global surveillance power this article is attributing to the Foundation. But that's just headcanon variance, I guess. Other than that, I'd say it's pretty solid, especially for a first article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic, it's unsettling without going all ooga-booga, it's fatal without being threatening, it avoids cliche, it's well conveyed, and it's just really damn interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really interesting debate and I have no intention of ruining it by giving too much away. But what I will say is that SCP-2055 is selecting people with quite specific characteristics. If those characteristics chime with your own values you'll probably feel the people selected represent the best of humanity, if those characteristics don't chime with your values you might feel very differently

There might well be unintentional punctuation errors I haven't noticed but the particular example you cite is a deliberate choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What is the best thing humanity has ever done?"

"Ah, good question. I'd say Hollywood, the way it used to be…or Paris, Paris is nice."

"What is the worst thing humanity has ever done?"

I dunno, the Holocaust I guess?

"What happens after you die?"

"I can't possibly know is the simple answer to that, but we all find out in time."

"What do you think is your own best quality?"

"Honesty."

"What do you think is your own worst quality?"

"Self-delusion."

"You don't seem particularly deluded."

"<laughing> I'll take that as a compliment!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'The best of humanity' is subjective, of course, but I would say that under my own criteria 1809 is a better person than 1207. I like the former's answers to what I imagine to be the questions 'What do you think is the most admirable characteristic in a person?' and 'What is the least?' near the end.

In any case, I got a sense of the threat of global population deterioration from this, and while you may read it differently, I really don't think it's wrong to read it the way I did.

Juries may only need 12 people, but the article strongly implies many more than 12 affectees: the numerical designation of the interviewees, the double-digit percentage of the global population, and the fact that if it was limited to 12 people it wouldn't really be Keter.

Author, after poring over the interviews more during my discussions with Voct, I've noticed that the punctuation is incorrect in a number of places. Personally, I think one should punctuate speech correctly, but I'm aware that it can be a matter of preference; I just wanted to ensure you are deliberately writing (for example) 'I'm a realist, I appreciate that there's good and bad in all of us' to indicate a certain speech pattern rather than the correct 'I'm a realist: I appreciate that there's good and bad in all of us'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… I don't know that they're choosing the best of humanity, either. What questions do you think they're answering?

Question #2 (after the preliminaries) is clearly "have you ever loved anyone", for instance.

Also, juries only need a small number of people (usually, though not necessarily, 12).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, I meant 'the gene pool' as a reference to the future of humanity generally. That is to say, if this SCP is killing/taking the best of humanity, we're going to end up all the worse off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think they're being chosen on genetic merits. It's about personality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely superb. Really neat idea, chilling implications (it's clearly only taking the best of us - so what's that going to do to the gene pool?), scary and dangerous with its wide reach without being over the top, flawless dialogue, and perfect execution generally. I greatly enjoyed trying to figure out what the questions were based on the two different responses, and I think you let just enough through for me to grasp what's going on. If I had one recommendation, it would be to remove the bit about voir dire because I thought that was a little bit too telling, but it makes sense for the Foundation to notice that it looks like legal proceedings and then consult experts, so by all means keep that in.

Altogether: heartfelt congratulations on your first SCP being so successful!

item.title: Re: TL;DR: The containment procedures are sloppy but the interview logs carry the article. +1
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to lay in my opinions against Soulless' here and say that

a) 'Best practice' is definitely correct and 'the best practice' is incorrect in this instance - they have subtly different meanings and should be used in slightly different contexts

b) A comma is not necessary where you have indicated, and 'authorised' is a perfectly legitimate spelling of the word

c) Methods of dealing with affected individuals are very much part of containment when the SCP is a phenomenon which affects large numbers of people

d) Agree with you about the redundancy in your penultimate point

e) I don't really think collapsing the logs is necessary. They're such an integral part of the piece. I think collapsing them will (incorrectly) project that all-too-familiar twist incoming feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this, the logs carry it quite well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirding this sentiment. Also adding that it warms the cockles of my heart to see such a well-executed first skip. =)

item.title: Re: TL;DR: The containment procedures are sloppy but the interview logs carry the article. +1
item.content:encoded: I almost always use the British spellings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes me wonder if this can happen with multiple people at once.

You know, that one time in school when the teacher asked you to stack chairs and no one did anything for like 2 minutes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the heads-up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You still have a Subject B in the image caption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is your first successful post, and it's Keter?

…I think we're going to get along great.

:D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, this is what made the transcripts work so well! Seconding this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having looked through dozens of eerie photographs of empty chairs in order to find the picture for this SCP I can definitely understand your fear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a percentage, I'll stick the % back in there.

item.title: Re: TL;DR: The containment procedures are sloppy but the interview logs carry the article. +1
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback, I'm not sure about using "the best practice", "best practice" is more common in policy/research papers and whether or not it is strictly grammatically correct I've often seen the phrase used at the start of a sentence and it scans fine to me.

Authorised is the correct spelling in english although I understand americans use "authorize" and its derivatives. Are US spelling prescribed on the site?

Thanks for the head up, will make the logs collapsible as they are fairly long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of you to say, I was aware when I was writing this that I was walking a fine line in terms of how much I gave away.

And thank you for the suggestion. I think you're definitely right, using "Subject A" and "Subject B" implies that these are comprehensive and/or sequential examples of SCP-2055 when I intended for them to be two illustrative "random" examples.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One-sided as they are, I found the transcripts to be unusually well-written. Normally they'd be all weird and stilted, basically just "What's that you say? An awkward word-for-word repetition of everything you just said so that the reader knows what's up?", and it's refreshing that you so successfully avoided that while still conveying enough for the exchanges to be informative and natural. They actually sound like something human beings might say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really great first showing. Oddly enough, I happen to have a lifelong fear of being alone with arrangements of empty chairs, so I've gotta upvote this one.

Also, I have to applaud you for having the process be one of jury selection instead of a defendant standing trial. You sidestepped the easy, obvious choice and found something much more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember reading this when it was in draft mode on the forums! It's really interesting, creepy, and pretty well written! Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've earned it, sir (Or woman.) For your first article, it's done better than i'd have to say about 75% of starting works on the site. In fact, i'd say it's done better than a few articles done by some longtime authors. Certainly better than anything I've tried to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd just like to thank everyone for their useful and generous responses, this is my first contribution to the site and it's very gratifying that people seem to have enjoyed it. I would also like to apologise for the self-tagging/upvoting and I hope you'll be patient with me as I find my feet.

I wasn't really sure how well this SCP was going to work, I was a bit concerned that the one-sided interview transcripts would read like gibberish and it has been pointed out to me that a suicide related SCP isn't exactly original. But I hope there's enough unusual elements to SCP-2055 to make it interesting and I'm going to try to build on the lessons gained from this in my next contribution.

I will respond to some specific points people have raised when I have the opportunity, but in the mean time I would like to thank you all once again for your feedback and I look forward to a long-term involvement with this community.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, it seems like some kind of jury of the soul. The person answers questions, and if the answers are right, then they hang themselves to go on to the next 'phase' of the interview or whatever.

I liked how innocuous this seemed in the description. There's just something charming about "chair-gathering". But once the logs describe everything, well, the whimsicality goes away but is replaced by much-needed meat. So I think it all works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't really get it. Can someone explain it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Observed in ███ of the global population

Is this a percentage? If so than you don't need to censor the %

item.title: TL;DR: The containment procedures are sloppy but the interview logs carry the article. +1
item.content:encoded:
Click for Full Review
Click to Hide

Best practice in relation to SCP-2055 is currently limited to constant monitoring of key personnel and the suppression of public knowledge.

Should be a 'the' in front of the sentence.

In the event of automated monitors being triggered the nearest available security officers should be alerted and authorised to carry out immediate physical intervention.

Should be a comma between triggered and the. Authorised is spelled Authorized.

In cases where remote monitoring has allowed SCP-2055 to be interrupted, affected individuals have become highly agitated and resistant.

This doesn't need to be in the containment procedures.

Once removed from the location of onset, abnormal behaviours cease, with the individual exhibiting no memory of preceding events.

Ditto this.

Due to the difficulty in predicting the occurrence, prevalence or individual outcomes of SCP-2055, constant surveillance is currently the only certain counter-measure.

This has already been stated as the first line in the containment procedures.

The logs should probably be put in collapsibles just for neatness.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Author, self-upvoting is not allowed on this site. Please remove your self-upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is strangely both frightening and philosophical.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. +1

Several individuals in a Senior Care and Protection Center beguine gathering chairs and mumbling incoherently to themselves. A tactical team intercepts the location and intervenes.
One of the individuals looks up and cheerfully asks, "here to join us for bingo?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is really weird. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is fantastic. I want to know so much more, which is always a good sign.

I do have one suggestion, though. Calling the people in the logs "Subject A" and "Subject B" carries with it some subtle implications that you probably don't intend. It makes it seem as if they were the only two people fully observed, or like they were somehow connected (like they were being tested for two different outcomes or something). It's a little hard to explain the kind of subtle connotations that terms can have, so I hope I'm communicating it well enough. The bottom line is, I would probably change them to something along the lines of "Subject 2055-17" and "Subject 2055-38."

Either way, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't find this SCP that interesting. Felt a bit 'meh' about it, didn't think I'd upvote or downvote. It was written well, but it just didn't grab me.

And then I got to the transcript logs, which were well written and quite interesting too! The exact same pause times at the beginnings, and the clever answers filled in a lot of the story of this SCP. The fact that the logs were both 1 sided conversations made it all the more impressive that they were so good.

+1 for excellent addendum

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ZombieYorick, please don't self-upvote, it's against the rules and generally considered to be in poor taste. Let your article stand up on its own merits!

Anyway, I kind of like this article. The transcript logs are a bit long but on the whole I think this is at least an interesting idea.
